stories.headline: Artemis 2 Is on the Pad — Humans Are Going Back to the Moon in 11 Days
stories.hook: For the first time since 1972, astronauts are days away from flying beyond low Earth orbit, and NASA's 322-foot rocket just rolled to its launch pad.
stories.body:
On March 20, NASA's Space Launch System rolled the two miles from the Vehicle Assembly Building to Launch Pad 39B at Kennedy Space Center — the same pad that launched Apollo 10. The sight of the 322-foot rocket and its orange Orion capsule silhouetted against the Florida sky marks the start of an 11-day countdown to what promises to be one of humanity's most ambitious missions in a generation.

Artemis 2, targeting a launch on April 1, will carry four astronauts — Commander Reid Wiseman, Pilot Victor Glover, Mission Specialist Christina Koch, and Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen — on a 10-day journey that loops around the Moon and returns to Earth. It will be the first crewed mission to fly beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 lifted off in December 1972. The crew will not land on the Moon, but will travel to within roughly 4,600 miles of the lunar surface, farther than any humans have ventured since the Apollo era.

The rollout follows months of intensive testing, hardware fixes, and weather delays that stretched the mission timeline well beyond its original schedule. Engineers resolved a faulty heat shield issue and several sensor anomalies that arose during Artemis 1's uncrewed 2022 test flight. The mission lays the groundwork for Artemis 3, which aims to land the first woman and first person of color on the Moon — a goal now within reach.

image.alt: Space shuttle on launch pad with NASA branding
image.credit: Photo by Sebastian Schuster on Unsplash

links.title: NASA: Artemis 2 Mission Overview
Space.com: Artemis 2 rolls to launch pad

stories.headline: Mars Is Hiding Water — Scientists Just Found Evidence of It Beneath the Surface
stories.hook: New research suggests liquid water may still exist beneath the Martian crust, a discovery that fundamentally changes the question of whether life could survive there today.
stories.body:
The story of Mars and water has always been past tense — billions of years ago, rivers carved its surface, lakes pooled in its basins, and oceans may have covered a third of the planet. New research published this month shifts that narrative to the present tense. Scientists analyzing seismic data from NASA's InSight lander have identified evidence of liquid water trapped in porous rock roughly 10 to 20 kilometers beneath the Martian surface — a vast subsurface reservoir with enough volume to fill an ancient ocean.

The finding is based on the way seismic waves slow down as they travel through certain underground layers, a signature consistent with liquid-saturated rock. While previous work suggested Mars lost most of its water to space or locked it in ice at the poles, these results indicate a third reservoir: a deep, pressurized zone where heat from the planet's interior keeps water in a liquid state.

The implications extend well beyond geology. Liquid water is the primary requirement for life as we know it, and deep subsurface environments on Earth — kilometers underground, far from sunlight — are home to microbial ecosystems that thrive in conditions once thought impossibly harsh. If Mars hosts a similar deep biosphere, either today or in the geologically recent past, the question of life on Mars takes on new urgency. Future missions that can probe deep below the surface are now a top priority.

image.alt: Mars projection map showing the planet's orange-red surface
image.credit: Photo by NASA Hubble Space Telescope on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Hidden water found beneath Mars surface
NASA InSight Mission

stories.headline: The World's Biggest Invertebrate Has Finally Been Filmed Alive in the Wild
stories.hook: Scientists just captured the first-ever underwater footage of a living colossal squid — a creature that can weigh over 500kg and has eyes the size of dinner plates.
stories.body:
Mesonychoteuthis hamiltoni — the colossal squid — has long existed at the edge of knowledge. Specimens have occasionally washed ashore or turned up in the stomachs of sperm whales, but until now, not a single living individual had ever been filmed in its natural deep-sea environment. That changed this month, when a remotely operated vehicle deployed during a deep-sea expedition to the Southern Ocean captured footage of a juvenile specimen at roughly 600 meters depth — glowing faintly, its tentacles outstretched, navigating the pitch-black water.

The colossal squid is the world's largest invertebrate by mass, potentially reaching 500 kilograms when fully grown, and it possesses the largest eyes of any animal on Earth — up to 27 centimeters across. Those enormous eyes are thought to be an adaptation for detecting the faint bioluminescence of predators like sperm whales in total darkness. Until now, everything scientists knew about the species came from dead or dying animals hauled to the surface.

The footage, described by researchers as "the most exciting marine biology moment of the decade," will allow scientists to study the animal's natural movement, behavior, and preferred depth range for the first time. The juvenile in the footage was estimated to be about 30 centimeters long — a reminder that the species, like so much of deep ocean life, remains almost entirely unknown to science. The team plans to return to the site with additional ROVs in hopes of filming a fully grown adult.

image.alt: Close-up of a deep sea creature glowing against a dark background
image.credit: Photo by Noé Sardet on Unsplash

links.title: Ocean Census: Colossal squid and new deep-sea species
Schmidt Ocean Institute: Deep sea exploration

stories.headline: The 12,800-Year Climate Mystery That Spawned a Comet Theory Has a New Answer
stories.hook: A mysterious cold snap that reshaped human civilization nearly 13,000 years ago was probably not triggered by an asteroid — and scientists think they finally know what actually happened.
stories.body:
Around 12,800 years ago, Earth was warming up from the last Ice Age when temperatures suddenly plummeted — triggering a cold period called the Younger Dryas that lasted 1,200 years, drove large mammals to extinction, and may have pushed early human populations to the brink. What caused it has been fiercely debated for decades. One popular theory pointed to a comet or asteroid impact, supported by a platinum anomaly found in Greenland ice cores that looked strikingly like a cosmic fingerprint.

New research published this week dismantles that theory. Scientists re-analyzed the ice cores in higher resolution and found the platinum spike doesn't mark a single catastrophic event — it shows a pattern consistent with a prolonged period of intense volcanic activity from multiple eruptions. The timing and distribution of the chemical signal matches known volcanic eruptions of the era far better than it fits the signature of an extraterrestrial impact.

The likely culprit: a cascade of large volcanic eruptions that injected sulfur dioxide into the stratosphere, blocking sunlight and triggering a feedback loop that froze large parts of the Northern Hemisphere. This mechanism — known as volcanic winter — is consistent with how other sudden cold periods have been triggered throughout Earth's history. The finding resolves one of the longest-running debates in Quaternary science, though some proponents of the impact hypothesis have already signaled they plan to contest the new data.

image.alt: A deep crack in ancient glacier ice against a cold sky
image.credit: Photo by Caio Portela on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Younger Dryas mystery solved
Quaternary Science Reviews

stories.headline: ADHD Brains Briefly Fall Asleep While You're Awake — Researchers Caught It Happening
stories.hook: Scientists have directly observed ADHD brains entering sleep-like states during demanding tasks, potentially explaining why focus feels genuinely impossible — not just difficult.
stories.body:
People with ADHD often describe their struggles with focus in terms that neurotypical people find hard to believe: not just "hard to pay attention" but a feeling that the brain simply drops offline at inconvenient moments. New research from a team of neuroscientists provides the first direct evidence that this experience is neurologically real. Using high-density EEG monitoring, the researchers tracked brain activity in participants with and without ADHD during sustained attention tasks — and found brief but measurable intrusions of sleep-like brain waves in ADHD participants, even when they were fully awake and trying hard to concentrate.

The phenomenon, called cortical slow-wave intrusions, lasted anywhere from a fraction of a second to several seconds. Critically, they appeared most frequently during cognitively demanding moments — precisely when attention is most needed. The same effect was minimal or absent in the control group performing identical tasks. Participants experiencing these intrusions showed measurably worse performance and reported higher perceived mental effort, suggesting the brain was working harder to maintain attention while periodically losing the battle.

The implications are significant for treatment. Current ADHD medications like stimulants primarily target dopamine and norepinephrine systems to improve alertness — a blunt tool if the underlying problem is intermittent cortical sleep intrusion. The researchers suggest that treatments targeting sleep architecture, sleep quality, or the specific neural circuits governing sleep-wake transitions might offer more targeted relief for many patients, opening a new avenue that goes beyond the dopamine-focused framework that has dominated ADHD treatment for decades.

image.alt: Abstract visualization of neural networks representing brain activity
image.credit: Photo by Alina Grubnyak on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: ADHD and sleep-like brain states
Journal of Neuroscience

stories.headline: The Shingles Vaccine Has a Surprise Benefit: It Cuts Heart Attack Risk by 46%
stories.hook: Scientists studying shingles vaccination outcomes stumbled onto something unexpected — the vaccine dramatically reduces major cardiac events, and nobody is entirely sure why.
stories.body:
The shingles vaccine has always had a straightforward pitch: prevent a painful, sometimes debilitating viral infection that affects roughly one in three adults over 60. New research is complicating that simple story in the best possible way. A large-scale analysis of health data from over 1.2 million adults found that people who received the shingles vaccine experienced a 46% reduction in major adverse cardiac events — including heart attack and stroke — within the following year. Among higher-risk subgroups, the reduction in all-cause mortality reached 66%.

The finding surprised even the researchers who discovered it. The most plausible explanation involves the connection between the varicella-zoster virus (which causes both chickenpox and shingles) and cardiovascular disease. The virus can reactivate inside blood vessel walls, causing chronic inflammation that accelerates atherosclerosis — the buildup of plaques that leads to heart attacks and strokes. Vaccination may suppress this reactivation, cooling down a long-term inflammatory process that quietly damages the cardiovascular system over years.

This adds shingles to a short but growing list of vaccines — including influenza and COVID-19 shots — that appear to confer cardiovascular benefits beyond their primary infectious disease purpose. The magnitude of the effect, if confirmed in randomized trials, would make the shingles vaccine one of the most cardioprotective medical interventions available. For the estimated 50% of adults over 50 who haven't yet been vaccinated, these findings add significant urgency to the already-strong recommendation to get the shot.

image.alt: A medical syringe prepared for a vaccine injection
image.credit: Photo by Sam Moghadam on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Shingles vaccine and heart health
CDC: Shingles vaccination recommendations

stories.headline: AI Found More Geothermal Energy Sites in 3 Years Than the Industry Found in 30
stories.hook: Machine learning models are locating clean geothermal energy deposits so much faster than traditional surveys that it's reshaping how the energy industry thinks about the entire clean power supply.
stories.body:
Geothermal energy — heat drawn from the Earth's interior — is arguably the most underrated clean energy source on the planet. It's always-on (unlike solar and wind), has a tiny land footprint, and the resource is theoretically almost unlimited. The catch has always been finding it: traditional geological surveys are slow, expensive, and hit-or-miss. That's now changing rapidly. AI-powered prospecting tools are identifying viable geothermal sites at a pace that would have been unimaginable a decade ago.

Startups and energy companies are deploying machine learning models trained on satellite imagery, seismic data, gravity and magnetic surveys, and historical well logs to identify subsurface conditions favorable for geothermal development. One company reports identifying more promising sites in the past three years than the entire industry catalogued in the previous three decades. The AI systems flag not just classic high-temperature volcanic sites, but also lower-temperature "enhanced geothermal systems" — deep hot rock that can be tapped almost anywhere on Earth with advanced drilling.

The technology is compressing the timeline from initial prospecting to operational power plant from 10-15 years to potentially 3-5 years. That acceleration, combined with breakthroughs in deep drilling technology, has attracted significant investment from both climate-focused funds and oil and gas companies pivoting their drilling expertise to a new energy source. Analysts now project that geothermal could supply up to 10% of global electricity by 2050 — a figure that would have seemed laughably optimistic just a few years ago.

image.alt: A large plume of white steam rising from geothermal vents in the ground
image.credit: Photo by Polina Lukianets on Unsplash

links.title: Fox Business: AI unlocks geothermal potential
US Department of Energy: Geothermal technologies

stories.headline: Three Men Charged With Smuggling $2.5 Billion in AI Chips to China
stories.hook: Federal prosecutors say the scheme routed cutting-edge NVIDIA chips through a web of shell companies in Taiwan and Southeast Asia — one of the largest technology export violations in US history.
stories.body:
Federal prosecutors announced charges this week against three individuals connected to the supply chain of Super Micro Computer, alleging a conspiracy to smuggle at least $2.5 billion worth of US artificial intelligence technology to China in violation of export control laws. The defendants allegedly routed high-end NVIDIA GPUs — the chips that power large AI models and are restricted from export to China under US national security regulations — through a network of front companies in Taiwan, Singapore, and Malaysia designed to obscure the chips' ultimate destination.

The case represents one of the largest alleged technology export violations in US history and highlights the difficulty of enforcing chip export controls in a global supply chain. US regulations, first imposed in 2022 and significantly tightened in 2024, prohibit the sale of advanced AI accelerators to Chinese entities on national security grounds. But the chips remain so valuable — and the downstream AI capabilities they enable so strategically important — that an entire shadow market has emerged to circumvent the rules.

Investigators say the scheme operated for approximately three years before being detected through a combination of financial surveillance, shipping records analysis, and cooperation from allied intelligence services. The indictment alleges the chips ultimately reached Chinese AI and defense research institutions. Legal experts say the charges signal a new phase in US enforcement of chip export controls — moving from corporate penalties to criminal prosecution of individual participants in smuggling networks, a shift that could significantly raise the stakes for future violations.

image.alt: Close-up tilt-shift photo of a green computer motherboard and chips
image.credit: Photo by Chris Ried on Unsplash

links.title: Al Jazeera: Three charged with smuggling AI chips
Reuters: US AI chip export controls explained

stories.headline: Astronomers May Have Watched a Magnetar Being Born Inside a Supernova for the First Time
stories.hook: Strange wobbles in the light from an unusually bright stellar explosion suggest a neutron star with a trillion-times-stronger magnetic field than Earth's was born in the blast.
stories.body:
Among the most extreme objects in the universe, magnetars hold a special place: neutron stars with magnetic fields so powerful — roughly a quadrillion times stronger than Earth's — that they deform the very atomic structure of matter around them. Despite their extraordinary nature, astronomers have never directly witnessed one being born. That may have just changed. Researchers analyzing the light curve of a supernova detected last year — designated SN 2024abfl — found peculiar oscillations and brightness spikes inconsistent with standard stellar explosion models.

When the team modeled what could produce those oscillations, a magnetar fit almost perfectly. The scenario: a massive star collapses, and instead of forming an ordinary neutron star, the rapid rotation and intense magnetic amplification of the collapse creates a magnetar spinning hundreds of times per second. The magnetar's powerful wind then pumps additional energy into the expanding explosion shell, producing the unusual brightness fluctuations the team observed.

The discovery, if confirmed, would give astronomers their first observational window into the birth of one of the universe's strangest objects. Magnetars are already known to emit the most powerful bursts of energy observed in the cosmos — giant flares that can release more energy in a fraction of a second than the Sun emits in 100,000 years. Understanding how they form is a key open question in high-energy astrophysics. The team plans follow-up observations with the James Webb Space Telescope to study the supernova remnant as it evolves over the coming years.

image.alt: A vivid red spiky stellar explosion against the darkness of space
image.credit: Photo by Logan Voss on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Magnetar born in supernova
NASA: What is a magnetar?

stories.headline: Cancer Cells Can Feel 10 Microns Ahead of Themselves — That's How They Navigate Tissue
stories.hook: New research shows individual tumor cells tug on the tissue around them to probe the path ahead, a discovery that could point to a new way to stop cancer from spreading.
stories.body:
When cancer spreads from a primary tumor to form metastases in distant organs, the individual cells making the journey face a navigation problem: they must push through dense, complex tissue, find paths between cells, and sense whether the route ahead is viable. Scientists have long known that cancer cells respond to mechanical cues in their environment, but the sensing range was assumed to be limited to the cell's immediate surface. New research reveals the sensing range is dramatically greater.

A team at MIT used ultra-sensitive force microscopy to measure how cancer cells interact with the collagen matrix surrounding them during migration. They found that individual cells extend microscopic protrusions called pseudopods that physically tug on collagen fibers up to 10 micrometers away — roughly the diameter of a red blood cell — gathering mechanical information about the landscape before the cell itself commits to moving in that direction.

The mechanism functions like a blind person using a cane: the cell probes the environment ahead, receives force feedback indicating density and stiffness, and adjusts its migration path accordingly. This explains how cancer cells navigate complex 3D tissue environments so effectively, and why tumors tend to spread along fibrous tissue highways rather than randomly in all directions. Drugs that interfere with the specific molecular machinery of this probing behavior — rather than just slowing cell movement in general — could potentially trap metastatic cancer cells in place before they establish new colonies elsewhere in the body.

image.alt: Colorized scanning electron micrograph of a lymphocyte cell on a blue background
image.credit: Photo by National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Cancer cells probe ahead
MIT News: Cancer cell navigation research

stories.headline: Monarch Butterflies Surged 64% This Year — Here's What Changed
stories.hook: After years of catastrophic decline, the orange-winged icons of North American migration are bouncing back, and scientists think they know which conservation efforts made the difference.
stories.body:
The Western monarch butterfly population hit its lowest recorded count in 2020, when surveyors at California overwintering sites counted fewer than 2,000 individuals — down from the 4.5 million counted in the 1980s. The trajectory looked like a one-way road to extinction. This month, the Xerces Society released its 2026 overwintering count: 87,000 monarchs, a 64% increase from last year and part of a multi-year recovery trend that is giving ecologists genuine reasons for cautious optimism.

Researchers credit a combination of factors for the rebound. Milkweed — the only plant monarch caterpillars can eat — has been actively replanted along migration corridors in the Midwest and West, partly through citizen science programs that recruited hundreds of thousands of garden and farmland owners. Climate conditions at key overwintering sites in California and Mexico were favorable over the past two winters, with fewer extreme weather events disrupting the delicate hibernation period. Reduced pesticide use in some agricultural regions may also have lowered larval mortality significantly.

The recovery remains fragile. 87,000 is still less than 2% of historical peak populations, and the threats — habitat loss, climate change, pesticide exposure — haven't gone away. Scientists warn that a single bad weather year, a drought drying out milkweed at critical points in the migration, or a warm winter disrupting the overwintering sites in Mexico could erase years of progress quickly. Still, after decades of grim news, the trend represents a genuine conservation success story in progress — proof that targeted intervention at multiple points in the lifecycle can make a measurable difference.

image.alt: A monarch butterfly perched on a flower with distinctive orange and black wings
image.credit: Photo by Steve Adams on Unsplash

links.title: Xerces Society: Western Monarch Count 2026
World Wildlife Fund: Monarch butterfly conservation

stories.headline: Scientists Built Smart Underwear That Counts Your Flatulence — The Average Is 32 Times a Day
stories.hook: A wearable sensor designed to monitor intestinal gas output is generating real data on gut health that questionnaires and lab tests could never provide.
stories.body:
Research into the gut microbiome has transformed our understanding of human health — linking the trillions of bacteria living in our intestines to everything from immune function to mental health. But studying gut gas production in real-world conditions has always been awkward. Lab tests capture snapshots; patient questionnaires are notoriously unreliable. A team of gastroenterologists and biomedical engineers solved the problem in the most direct way possible: they built underwear equipped with a hydrogen gas sensor and a Bluetooth transmitter, designed to continuously monitor intestinal gas output throughout the day.

In early clinical trials, participants wore the device for two weeks while going about their normal lives. The sensor detected hydrogen — a byproduct of bacterial fermentation of undigested carbohydrates — and logged each measurable event with a timestamp. The results were illuminating: the average participant experienced roughly 32 detectable flatulence events per day, significantly more than the 14-25 range typically cited in medical literature, which is based on clinical studies where participants behave differently than they would at home.

Beyond settling the "how many times a day" question, the device is generating rich data about which foods trigger elevated gas production in different individuals, how gut microbiome composition correlates with gas patterns, and how conditions like irritable bowel syndrome and lactose intolerance manifest in continuous monitoring. The researchers are now developing a version that can distinguish between different gas types — hydrogen, methane, and hydrogen sulfide — to provide a fuller picture of what's happening in the human gut.

image.alt: Students performing experiments in a university science laboratory
image.credit: Photo by Trnava University on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Smart underwear gut health research
Gut journal: Microbiome and gas production

stories.headline: Hedgehogs Can Hear Ultrasound — and That Discovery Might Save Their Lives
stories.hook: Scientists discovered that the humble garden hedgehog hears frequencies well above the human range, a finding with an immediate and practical conservation application.
stories.body:
Hedgehogs are in serious trouble across Europe. The UK population has declined by roughly a third in the past decade, with road deaths one of the leading causes of mortality — hundreds of thousands are killed by vehicles every year. Researchers at the University of Exeter set out to understand hedgehog hearing as part of a broader effort to develop deterrents that could warn the animals away from roads. What they found went well beyond what the team expected.

Using auditory brainstem response testing — a technique that measures electrical signals in the brain in response to sounds — the researchers demonstrated that hedgehogs can hear frequencies up to at least 45 kilohertz, well into the ultrasonic range that is imperceptible to humans. Previously, hedgehogs were assumed to have fairly standard mammalian hearing. The ultrasonic hearing capability is thought to help them locate insects and other prey in dense vegetation, where visual cues are limited. The team also found that hedgehog hearing is most sensitive in the 8-16 kHz range — slightly higher than the range where human hearing peaks.

The discovery has an immediate practical application: if hedgehogs can hear ultrasound, ultrasonic road deterrents — devices already used for deer and small mammals in some countries — might be adapted to work for them. Installing such devices along roadsides near hedgehog population hotspots could significantly reduce vehicle collisions. More broadly, the research opens new avenues for understanding hedgehog communication, which remains poorly studied, and for developing non-invasive methods to track and monitor hedgehog populations in the wild.

image.alt: A hedgehog sitting on the ground showing its distinctive prickly spines
image.credit: Photo by Amir hossein on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Hedgehogs hear ultrasound
University of Exeter: Hedgehog research

stories.headline: The Small Magellanic Cloud Is a Wreck — Because It Crashed into Our Galaxy's Neighbor
stories.hook: One of the two dwarf galaxies visible to the naked eye from Earth is a chaotic mess of stars, and astronomers have finally confirmed why: it survived a catastrophic collision millions of years ago.
stories.body:
When astronomers look at the Small Magellanic Cloud — one of two dwarf galaxies orbiting just 200,000 light years from the Milky Way — they see something puzzling: an irregular, asymmetric galaxy with stars moving in unexpected directions and unusual filaments of gas stretching across it like scars. For decades, the cause of this chaos was unclear. New research using data from the European Space Agency's Gaia spacecraft has settled the question.

The culprit is the Large Magellanic Cloud — the Small Magellanic Cloud's larger companion. The two galaxies have been gravitationally bound for billions of years, but simulations now show that a direct collision between them occurred roughly 300 to 500 million years ago. The encounter was violent enough to strip stars from their original orbits, trigger massive waves of star formation, and deform the Small Magellanic Cloud's structure into the chaotic form we see today. Gaia's precise measurements of stellar velocities — tracking the three-dimensional motion of millions of individual stars — provided the smoking gun that distinguishes the collision scenario from alternative explanations.

The finding reshapes our understanding of how small galaxies evolve. Rather than evolving quietly under the steady influence of gravity, dwarf galaxies can experience violent, transformative collisions that leave scars visible hundreds of millions of years later. The Small Magellanic Cloud is essentially still reverberating from the impact — a reminder that even objects on cosmic scales carry the history of their most dramatic moments.

image.alt: Two spiral galaxies colliding in deep space, captured by the Hubble Space Telescope
image.credit: Photo by NASA Hubble Space Telescope on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Small Magellanic Cloud ancient collision
ESA Gaia mission

stories.headline: Scientists Found a Carnivorous 'Death-Ball' Sponge Lurking at the Bottom of the Southern Ocean
stories.hook: Among 30 newly discovered deep-sea species, one stands out: a sponge that traps and digests small animals using a sticky, spherical grip that has no parallel in the known biological record.
stories.body:
The deep Southern Ocean, plunging to nearly 6,000 meters below the surface, is one of the least explored environments on Earth. A recent expedition by the Ocean Census initiative returned from a survey of seamounts and abyssal plains south of Argentina with 30 species never previously described by science — including something that stopped taxonomists cold: a spherical, spiky sponge that appears to be carnivorous, which the research team immediately nicknamed the "death-ball."

Most sponges are filter feeders, passively drawing water through their bodies and extracting bacteria and organic particles. Carnivorous sponges — a category discovered only in the 1990s — use hook-like filaments to snare small crustaceans and slowly digest them. The death-ball appears to be a more aggressive variant: its rounded shape and dense array of spicules (glass-like mineral fibers) seem designed to envelop and trap prey rather than simply ensnare it. In video footage captured by the expedition's ROV, the sponge shows unusual retraction behavior when something brushes against it — unlike anything documented in other sponge species.

Beyond the death-ball, the haul included new species of sea cucumbers, brittle stars, polychaete worms, and an unusually large amphipod crustacean. The Ocean Census, a global initiative launched in 2023 with the goal of discovering 100,000 new marine species by 2030, notes that fewer than 20% of ocean species have been formally described by science. At current discovery rates, most of the ocean's biodiversity will remain unnamed and unstudied for generations.

image.alt: Close-up of gorgonian coral polyps extended to catch food on the ocean floor
image.credit: Photo by Erick Morales Oyola on Unsplash

links.title: Ocean Census: Carnivorous death-ball sponge and 30 new deep-sea species
Natural History Museum: Deep sea research
